Click + Add Subaccount and fill in:

 

Field

Value

Region

    ap21 (for Singapore - Azure)

Subaccount ID

        Your BTP Subaccount ID

Display Name

         Any name e.g. My Trial

Subaccount User

        Your BTP login email

Password

         Your BTP password

Location ID

    Multiple cloud connector address

NOTE: The Location ID is a unique identifier used when you want to connect multiple Cloud Connectors to the same BTP Subaccount.

  • It is crucial when multiple Cloud Connectors connect to one BTP subaccount.

Note: Where to find your Subaccount ID?
BTP Cockpit → Click your subaccount → Overview page → Subaccount ID field

 

After saving, you should see:

Tunnel Information → Status: Connected

 

Step 6 — Verify in BTP Cockpit

Go to BTP Cockpit → Connectivity → Cloud Connectors

Enter your Location ID in the provided field and click to verify the connection.

 

You should see your Cloud Connector listed with status Connected.

 

This confirms the tunnel between your laptop and BTP is established.

Step 7 — Expose a Local System (Optional Practice)

To practice further, you can expose a local server through Cloud Connector.

Example: Rebex Tiny SFTP Server

  • Download from: https://www.rebex.net/tiny-sftp-server/
  • Run it : it starts an SFTP server on localhost:22

 

  • Click Start . the SFTP server will begin running.

In Cloud Connector → Cloud to On-Premises → + (Add Mapping):

  

 

Field

Value

Back-End Type

            Non-SAP System

Protocol

              TCP

Internal Host

       localhost

Internal Port

       22

NOTE : Internal host and virtual host should be different                                                                   

 

After saving, the Check Result should show: reachable

 

This means BTP can now reach your local SFTP server securely through the tunnel.

Key Concepts Summary

Concept

Explanation

Reverse Invoke Proxy

          On-premise initiates connection, not cloud

Virtual Host

          Fake address BTP uses . Real address stays hidden

Internal Host

          Actual address of your on-premise system

Location ID

        Used when multiple Cloud Connectors connect to same subaccount

Tunnel

        Secure TLS channel between Cloud Connector and BTP

Subaccount Certificate

          Auto-generated certificate to authenticate the connection

Common Mistakes to Avoid

  1. Closing the CMD window : Cloud Connector stops if you close 
<go.bat>​

 window .

  • Not restarting CMD: After setting Java environment variables, always open a fresh Command Prompt window. Otherwise the system will not recognize the newly added variables.

What's Next?

Now that Cloud Connector is connected to BTP, you can:

  • Create a Destination in BTP pointing to your on-premise system
  • Build an iFlow in SAP CPI that reads/writes files to your SFTP server through Cloud Connector
  • Practice real on-premise to cloud and cloud to on-premise data transfer scenarios

I will cover these in my next blog post.

References

  • SAP Official Cloud Connector Documentation 
  • SAP Tools Download Page

I’m a beginner documenting my learning journey as I go. Since I’m still practicing, please let me know in the comments if you spot any mistakes or have tips to share! Let's learn together.

entry.title: Como integrar um serviço BTP Cloud Foundry a um ambiente S4HANA On-premise
entry.content:
O cenário é o seguinte: você quer hospedar um app Fiori Freestyle no BTP Cloud Foundry, que vai se conectar a um sistema S4HANA on-premise e será utilizado pelos usuários finais.

Pode parecer bem específico, mas isso envolve um entendimento de várias camadas de arquitetura, então vale a pena tomar um tempo para entender isso.

Esse objetivo envolve criar serviços auxiliares, que vão se comunicar com o S4HANA por intermédio do Cloud Connector.

O primeiro passo é instalar o SAP Cloud Connector, pode ser no mesmo servidor que hospeda o S4HANA ou em outro servidor na mesma rede.

Ele é um programa Java, então é necessário instalar a JVM. Tecnicamente pode ser usada qualquer JVM, porém a SAP também possui suas próprias versões da JVM, então é recomendável usar elas. Os detalhes estão nesse link: https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/installation.

Com ele rodando no ambiente, você vai configurar os dados da sua BTP subaccount (ele pode se conectar em mais de uma subaccount).

A conexão entre os serviços da subaccount e o S4HANA vão ser intermediadas por essa conexão com o Cloud Connector.

Quando falamos em integração de serviços externos com um ambiente SAP, não podemos ignorar a parte de autenticação e segurança. Existem várias formas de configuração, mas a mais “nativa” para esse cenário seria a Principal Propagation.

Para entender a arquitetura, mentalize o seguinte fluxo, supondo que no Cloud Foundry você escolha o Node como backend:

  • O usuário abre a aplicação Fiori, que tenta puxar uma informação de uma API do S4HANA através do path relativo (Ex: /sap/opu/odata4/sap/z*);

  • Essa requisição cai no container Node, e se o usuário não estiver logado, ele é redirecionado para uma url de autenticação, onde é informado seu email e senha do Identity Provider (que pode ser da SAP, Azure, AWS, etc.);

  • Essa autenticação gera um token com informações do usuário, que é enviado ao Node;

  • O Node, tendo o token e o caminho da API, direciona ambos para um destination, que aponta para a conexão criada no Cloud Connector;

  • O Cloud Connector pega uma informação desse token, por exemplo o e-mail do usuário, e gera um certificado digital temporário;

  • Esse certificado é enviado em uma conexão https para o endpoint real do S4HANA

  • O S4HANA valida o certificado e mapeia ele para um usuário interno

  • A partir daí a lógica da API odata segue normal, baseada no que o usuário pode fazer, e a resposta é devolvida;

Existe um outro blog post que também explica sobre o Principal Propagation. De qualquer forma vou explicar aqui, mas se quiser conferir, o link é: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/principal-propagation-setup-between-s4hana-and-btp-portal/ba-p/13522753.

Para tornar isso possível, após conectar o Cloud Connector na subaccount, o próximo passo vai ser configurar os certificados digitais dele, na área “Connector > Configuration > On-premises”.

Nesse painel existem 2 tipos de certificado digital: um é para o proxy do Cloud Connector, esse é denominado “System certificate”, e o outro é o que o Cloud Connector utiliza para gerar os certificados temporários, denominado “CA Certificate”.

Nessa mesma página é possível configurar quais informações extrair do token para gerar o certificado, na área do Principal Propagation.

Após isso, é hora de configurar a conexão com o S4HANA on-premise.

Primeiramente, o Cloud Connector precisa confiar no certificado https do servidor S4HANA. Por padrão ele só confia em uma lista definida de certificados, denominada “Back-end Trust Store”. Você pode fazer lá o upload manualmente, ou pode também desativar essa opção, assim ele vai confiar em qualquer certificado do back-end.

Feito isso, na área da sua subaccount, página “Cloud to On-Premises”, vai ser configurado os detalhes específicos do proxy.

A conexão deve ser necessariamente via HTTPS, pois é assim que o Cloud Connector consegue trocar o certificado gerado com o S4HANA de forma nativa através do próprio protocolo.

Exemplo (na edição ele exibe como HTTP, mas foi criada como HTTPS):

Feito isso, a próxima etapa é fazer o Cloud Connector confiar no token gerado pelo serviço de autenticação da sua subaccount.

Para isso, nessa mesma página do “Cloud to On-Premises” existe também a aba do “Principal propagation”, onde você deve sincronizar. A primeira execução é manual, depois dela você pode marcar a opção para sincronizar automaticamente.

 

Em resumo, até aqui já temos uma instância do Cloud Connector rodando na rede, vinculada a uma subaccount BTP, com os certificados system e CA criados, e configurada para se conectar ao S4HANA on-premise via https.

Agora faltam 2 coisas: configurar o S4HANA para confiar no Cloud Connector, e configurar os serviços no BTP Cloud Foundry para que seja possível essa integração.

Para que o S4HANA confie no Cloud Connector, é necessário ajustar alguns parâmetros de perfil do sistema (isso envolve configurações BASIS).

O primeiro ponto é fazer ele confiar no certificado digital de sistema do Cloud Connector, existem 2 formas de fazer isso:

  • Com os antigos parâmetros “icm/HTTPS/trust_client_with_subject” e “icm/HTTPS/trust_client_with_issuer”, que são configurados via transação RZ10. Esses parâmetros definem o padrão do emissor e assunto do certificado digital que o sistema pode confiar, e é um valor único. Eles são do tipo estático, então é necessário reiniciar a instância do S4 para aplicar as edições.

Exemplo:

  • Com o novo parâmetro “icm/trusted_reverse_proxy_<x>”, onde o “<x>” é um número sequencial começando do 0. Ele tem esse padrão porque dessa forma é possível configurar mais de um certificado confiado pelo servidor, diferente dos anteriores que só permitiam um valor. Ele é do tipo dinâmico, então não é sempre necessário reiniciar a instância para aplicar, e pode ser alterado a partir da transação RZ11 (de forma indireta). Mais informações sobre ele no link: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_NETWEAVER_AS_ABAP_752/077f95b579a0474fbef5d520be10b680/3bea0adde9a54db5843be8f7bf01fefa.html.

Exemplo:

Também é necessário importar os dois certificados do Cloud Connector na transação STRUST, no servidor SSL Padrão.

Feito isso, o sistema S4HANA confia no certificado do Cloud Connector, mas ainda não consegue mapear os certificados temporários para usuários dentro do SAP. Aí entra a configuração da transação CERTRULE, onde você faz o upload de um certificado temporário gerado pelo Cloud Connector e pode mapear ele para usuários através de regras, ou diretamente.

Exemplo:

Para habilitar isso também é necessário configurar um parâmetro de perfil, o “login/certificate_mapping_rulebased”:

No lado do S4HANA é basicamente isso, agora só falta configurar os serviços dentro do BTP.

O cenário pensado nesse tutorial é de uma aplicação CAP Fiori rodando no Cloud Foudry, então vamos criar os serviços no mesmo space da aplicação.

São basicamente 3 serviços:

  • destination: Conceito similar ao da transação SM59, guarda dentro dele o destino para a conexão do Cloud Connector. Ele possui painel próprio para configurações.

  • connectivity: É um serviço sem configuração nenhuma, que apenas serve como ponte entre a aplicação e o serviço de destination.

  • xsuaa: É o que faz a ponte da aplicação com o provedor de identidade. Ele é configurado apenas via json, onde é possível definir o app que vai consumi-lo, as urls que pode redirecionar após autenticar o usuário, e até mesmo criar novas roles dentro do BTP, que podem posteriormente ser incluídas em Role collections e atribuídas a usuários.

Feito isso, só falta configurar a conexão dentro do painel do “Destination Service”. Lá você vai criar um destination com o Proxy type “OnPremise” e mapear para a url virtual gerada no painel do Cloud Connector, com autenticação via “PrincipalPropagation”.

Exemplo:

O app node deve estar configurado para que o destino do S4HANA seja esse Name que você configurou acima (não confunda isso com a url virtual ou com o serviço destination).

Agora para integrar tudo isso, basta a aplicação estar configurada para fazer o binding nesses 3 serviços. Isso é feito através do arquivo “manifest.yml”, usado na hora de subir o app no Cloud Foundry.

Exemplo:

Finalmente, seu app Node estará pronto para se autenticar e consumir serviços odata do seu S4HANA On-Premise.

 

Em resumo, ao seguir esse tutorial você foi capaz de entender e configurar as camadas de integração entre um app no BTP Cloud Foundry e um sistema S4Hana On-Premise (via api OData).

Talvez essas ferramentas, configurações e caminhos específicos mudem com o tempo, o mais importante é entender a arquitetura e os conceitos envolvidos nessa integração.

Obrigado pela leitura, espero ter ajudado 😄

entry.title: End to End Guide: Enabling SAP Joule in SAP S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad (PCE + OnPremise PoC)
entry.content:
1. Introduction

SAP Joule is SAP's generative AI, designed to help users accomplish tasks through natural language interaction within SAP applications. This guide will provide a step by step walkthrough to enable joule in SAP Fiori.

The guide is primarily written for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition (PCE).
PS: Also covers steps that differ for On-Premise S/4HANA (for a Proof-of-Concept environment).

2. Component Overview

Before starting, it is important to understand the key components involved and the role each plays.

Component

What It Is

Role in This Integration

SAP Joule

SAP's generative AI copilot. A cloud service on SAP BTP.

The AI assistant end users interact with. Loaded in FLP header as a Shell Plugin.

SAP BTP

SAP Business Technology Platform  the cloud foundation.

Hosts Joule, Work Zone, Identity Services, and destinations connecting to S/4HANA.

SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

SAP's digital workplace platform. Can serve as launchpad or content bridge.

Acts as the technical bridge between Joule and S/4HANA. Required in the Joule Formation.

SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS)

SAP's Identity Authentication Service. Manages user identity and SSO.

Provides the SSO layer. All BTP services require IAS authentication.

SAP Cloud Connector (SCC)

An on-premise agent creating a secure tunnel from BTP to on-premise systems.

Enables Work Zone and Joule on BTP to securely reach S/4HANA.

SAP Fiori Launchpad (FLP)

The S/4HANA web UI hub where users launch Fiori apps.

The surface where the Joule icon appears via a Shell Plugin target mapping.

Formation

A BTP System Landscape construct grouping systems for a specific integration.

Tells Joule which Work Zone, IAS tenant, and S/4HANA belong together.

CDM3

Common Data Model v3 . SAP Fiori's content model (catalogs, tiles, groups).

Used to expose S/4HANA Fiori content to BTP so Work Zone can sync it.

3. Architecture Overview

The following describes the end-to-end integration flow:

  • End User opens S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad in browser
  • FLP authenticates via IAS (after SSO is configured)
  • FLP loads the Joule Shell Plugin from the Joule Web Client (sapdas.cloud.sap)
  • Joule on BTP connects to Work Zone via the Joule Formation
  • Work Zone reaches S/4HANA via BTP Destinations → Cloud Connector → ABAP Server(S/4 system)
  • Joule icon appears in FLP header. Users interact with Joule through natural language.

4. Prerequisites

Requirement

Details

SAP BTP Global Account

Admin access. Subaccount in the same region as your IAS tenant.

SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS)

IAS tenant associated with your BTP Global Account.

SAP S/4HANA System

PCE or On-Premise (for PoC). FLP accessible via browser.

SAP Cloud Connector

Installed and running. Registered to your BTP subaccount.

S/4HANA Technical User

User with access to CDM3 service (/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3) and FLP admin roles.

BTP Entitlements

Joule (foundation), SAP Build Work Zone standard edition, SAP Cloud Identity Services.

Regional Alignment

BTP subaccount, IAS tenant, and Work Zone must be in the same region.

Note: PCE vs On-Premise: PCE uses token-based registration via Communication Arrangements. On-Premise uses Cloud Connector and manual destinations. Each step calls out differences.

5. Step-by-Step Guide

Step 1: Enable Cloud Foundry in BTP Subaccount

Why: Cloud Foundry is the runtime where BTP service instances (Work Zone, Joule) are deployed. Without it, subscriptions and the Joule booster cannot run.

  1. Open SAP BTP Cockpit → navigate to your target Subaccount → Overview.
  2. Click Enable Cloud Foundry. Accept defaults (Plan: standard). Click Create.
  3. Once enabled, click Create Space → name it e.g. 'dev'. Assign yourself Space Developer + Space Manager roles.

Validation: Subaccount Overview shows CF Enabled, Org Name, API Endpoint, and space 'dev' listed.

On-Premise PoC: No difference. This step is identical for both PCE and On-Premise.

 

Step 2: Add Entitlements

Why: Entitlements grant your subaccount the right to use specific BTP services. Without them, the services cannot be subscribed to or instantiated.

  1. Go to Global Account → Entitlements → Entity Assignments.
  2. Select your subaccount → Configure Entitlements → Add Service Plans.
  3. Add: Joule (also search as 'das-application') — plan: foundation
  4. Add: SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition — plan: standard
  5. Add: SAP Cloud Identity Services — plan: connectivity
  6. Click Save.

Validation: All three services appear in Entitlements list with quota assigned.

Step 3: Establish Trust to IAS

Why: All BTP services (including Joule) authenticate via IAS. Establishing trust allows your subaccount to delegate authentication to IAS, enabling SSO across all connected services.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Security → Trust Configuration.
  2. Click Establish Trust.
  3. Select your IAS tenant (e.g., yourtenantid.accounts.ondemand.com).
  4. Click Next through the wizard → Finish.

Validation: Trust Configuration shows your IAS tenant with status Active.

Step 4: Configure Trusted Domains in BTP

Why: BTP Trusted Domains control which browser origins can interact with your subaccount's services via iFrames and cross-origin calls. Without this, the Joule panel cannot be embedded in your FLP.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Security → Settings → Trusted Domains.
  2. Click Add for each of the following origins (scheme + host + port if non-standard):
  • Your FLP origin: e.g. https://your-s4-host:44301
  • Your Joule Web Client: https://<tenant>.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap
  • Your Work Zone site: https://<site-id>.launchpad.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
  1. Click Save.

Note: Your Joule URL is found in System Landscape → Formations → your Joule formation → URL column of the Joule system entry.(You can maintain it later once Joule is activated via booster etc.)

Validation: All required origins appear in the Trusted Domains list.

Step 5: Configure Trusted Domains in IAS

Why: IAS maintains its own trusted domain allowlist. Cross-domain authentication requires IAS to trust the same origins as BTP.

  1. Open IAS Admin Console: https://<ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/admin
  2. Go to Applications & Resources → Tenant Settings → Customization → Trusted Domains.
  3. Click Add — enter hostname only (no scheme or port) for each host.
  4. Click Save.

Note: Format difference: BTP accepts full origins (https://host:44301). IAS accepts hostname only (host.example.com).

Validation: IAS shows 'Trusted Domains updated' confirmation.

Step 6: Expose SAP Fiori Launchpad Content (CDM3)

Why: Work Zone needs to know what Fiori apps and catalogs exist in S/4HANA. The CDM3 export makes this content discoverable so Work Zone can sync it.

Pre-requisite: Activate CDM3 ICF Service

  1. In S/4HANA, run transaction SICF.
  2. Navigate to /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3. If inactive, right-click → Activate Service.

Expose Content

  1. Run transaction /n/UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE.
  2. Select Version 1.
  3. Choose All roles (first-time setup) or select specific roles.
  4. Click Expose Content in the toolbar. Wait for completion.
  5. Click View Log to verify results.

Note: Warnings in the CDM3 log are normal. Red errors should be investigated but typically do not block the integration.

Validation: Log shows entities exposed (e.g., '14,663 out of 14,671 entities exposed'). Click View Exposed Content to confirm.

Step 7: Set Up SAP Cloud Connector

Why: Cloud Connector creates a secure outbound-only tunnel from your on-premise/PCE network to BTP. Work Zone and Joule use this tunnel to reach S/4HANA without inbound firewall rules.

Please setup cloud connector first.

7.1 Register BTP Subaccount

  1. Open SCC Admin UI (e.g. https://localhost:8443).
  2. Add your BTP subaccount using Download Authentication Data from BTP → Connectivity → Cloud Connectors.
  3. Confirm status shows Connected (green).

7.2 Create Back-End Mapping

  1. Go to Cloud to On-Premises → + Add:
  • Protocol: HTTPS (preferred) or HTTP (PoC)
  • Internal Host/Port: e.g. S/4HANA internal hostname + HTTPS port 44301 or HTTP port 8001
  • Virtual Host: a clean alias (e.g., s4hana-virtual)
  • Back-End Type: ABAP System
  1. Add Resources: /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3, /sap/bc/ui2/flp, /sap/bc/ui5_ui5 — all with 'Path and All Sub-Paths'.

Validation: Mapping shows green 'Reachable' status. BTP Cockpit → Connectivity → Cloud Connectors shows 'Available'.

On-Premise PoC: If HTTPS port 44301 is blocked between SCC and ABAP server, use HTTP port 8001 for PoC. In SICF, navigate to /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3 → Logon Data tab → check 'Use all logon procedures' to allow Basic Authentication.

Step 8: Activate Subscriptions and Instances

Why: Subscriptions activate the actual services in your subaccount. Without subscribing to Work Zone and Joule, neither the Joule icon nor the content bridge can function.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Instances and Subscriptions → Create.
  2. Subscribe to SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition — plan: standard.
  3. Subscribe to Joule (das-application) — plan: foundation.
  4. For Work Zone: also create a Service Instance (name: BuildZone) and a Service Key.
  5. Launch Work Zone from Instances & Subscriptions at least once — this registers it in System Landscape.

Note: The Joule Booster (next step) looks for the Work Zone subscription in its dropdown. It only appears after you launch Work Zone at least once.

Validation: Subscriptions show 'Subscribed'. Service Key created. System Landscape → Systems shows Work Zone auto-registered.

Step 9: Configure the Joule Formation

Why: A Formation is a logical grouping that tells Joule which Work Zone, IAS tenant, and S/4HANA belong together. Without a formation, Joule has no context about which systems to connect to.

Option A — Run the Joule Booster (Recommended for PCE)

  1. Go to Global Account → Boosters → search 'Setting Up Joule' → click Start.
  2. Wizard: Select subaccount and CF space (dev).
  3. Step 3 — Select Integrations: choose SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.
  4. Step 4 — Select Capabilities: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition + SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.
  5. Step 5 — Select your Work Zone system and S/4HANA system.
  6. Click Execute and wait for completion.

Option B — Add Systems to Existing Formation

  1. Go to Global Account → System Landscape → Formations.
  2. Find the formation of type 'Integration with Joule' with status Ready.
  3. Click Include Systems → add your Work Zone instance and S/4HANA system.
  4. Confirm integration options (Enable Capability Deployment, Enable Joule Icon).

Validation: Formation shows status Ready (green). Includes IAS, Work Zone, and Joule systems. Note the Joule URL — needed for Trusted Domains and shell plugin.

On-Premise PoC: On-Premise systems cannot complete Communication Arrangement token registration.

Recommended approach:

(1) Run the booster selecting only Work Zone as the integration (not S/4HANA).
(2) Register S/4HANA manually in System Landscape → Systems → Add → Type: SAP S/4HANA Cloud. (Not needed for POC)

Step 10: Configure BTP Destinations

Why: BTP Destinations define how Work Zone and Joule connect to S/4HANA. Without correct destinations, Work Zone cannot read content and Joule cannot reach the backend.

Create the following destination (Design-Time and Runtime can be the same for PoC):

Property

Value

Name

e.g, jouletc  (lowercase, no underscores)

Type

HTTP

URL

http://<scc-virtual-host>:<port>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3

Proxy Type

OnPremise

Authentication

BasicAuthentication

User / Password

S/4HANA technical user with CDM3 access

sap-client

Your S/4HANA client (e.g., 100)

HTML5.DynamicDestination

true

WebIDEEnabled

true

WebIDEUsage

odata_abap

 

Critical: Work Zone Destination Naming: Work Zone only accepts destination names that are lowercase with no underscores. Names like MCF_TC will cause an error. Use names like jouletc or s4hana.

Critical: URL Path: The destination URL must include the CDM3 path: /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3. Without this, Work Zone receives an HTML login page instead of JSON, resulting in an authorization error.

Validation: Check Connection returns HTTP 200. e.g. SWZ_JOULE destination is auto-created and visible in the destinations list.

 

Step 11: Configure Work Zone Channel Manager

Why: The Channel Manager connects Work Zone to S/4HANA as a Content Provider. This sync imports S/4HANA's Fiori catalogs and makes the app landscape available to Joule.

  1. Launch SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition from Instances & Subscriptions.
  2. Go to Channel Manager → + New.
  3. Fill in:
  • Title: your system name (e.g., S4H_UIB)
  • Design-Time Destination: your lowercase destination (e.g., jouletc)
  • Runtime Destination: same
  • Content Scope: Roles and related content
  • Automatically add all content items: ON
  1. Click Save, then click the sync icon.

Wait 5-15 minutes for first sync

On-Premise PoC: The channel may show 'Updated' with a 'Connectivity Error' in the Report. This error is for real-time change notification callbacks, which on-premise systems do not support. The content sync itself succeeds. Re-sync manually when S/4HANA content changes.

Validation: Channel shows Updated status. Content Manager → Explore confirms S/4HANA catalogs are visible.

Step 12: Configure Joule Shell Plugin in S/4HANA FLP

Why: The Joule icon in FLP is delivered via a SAPUI5 Shell Plugin configured as a Target Mapping in an FLP catalog. Without this step, users will not see the Joule icon regardless of BTP configuration.

12.1 Create Technical Catalog

  1. In S/4HANA, run transaction /n/UI2/FLPD_CUST (Launchpad Content Manager: Cross-Client).
  2. In the Catalogs tab, create a new catalog: ID = Z_JOULE_TECH, Title = 'Joule Technical Catalog'. Package: $TMP (PoC).

12.2 Create Shell Plugin Target Mapping

  1. Open catalog Z_JOULE_TECH in the Launchpad Designer (opens in browser).
  2. Click Target Map... tab → Create Target Mapping.
  3. Fill in:
  • Semantic Object: Shell
  • Action: plugin  (type manually — not in dropdown)
  • Application Type: SAPUI5 Fiori App
  • URL: https://<joule-tenant>.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap/resources/public/webclient/s4  (NO trailing slash)
  • Title: Joule
  • ID: sap.das.webclientplugin.s4
  • Save.

Critical: No Trailing Slash: The Joule Web Client URL must NOT end with a slash. Use .../webclient/s4 not .../webclient/s4/. A trailing slash causes 'File not found' and the icon will not appear.

Note: How to find your Joule URL: Go to System Landscape → Formations → your Joule formation. The Joule system shows a URL like https://<tenant>.eu10.sapdas.cloud.sap. Append /resources/public/webclient/s4 to this base URL.

12.3 Create Role and Assign Catalog

  1. Run transaction PFCG → create single role Z_JOULE_S4HANA.
  2. Menu tab → click Launchpad Catalog → assign Z_JOULE_TECH (Local Front-End Server).
  3. Authorizations tab → Change Authorization Data → Do not select templates → Generate → Save.
  4. User tab → assign role to target users → User Comparison → Full Comparison → OK.

Validation: Log in to FLP. The Joule icon (sparkle/diamond) appears in the FLP header. Clicking it opens the Joule panel.

Step 13: Configure IAS SSO for S/4HANA FLP (Recommended)

Why: Joule is secured by IAS. If FLP uses ABAP local login, users must log in again when Joule opens. Configuring IAS SSO for FLP gives a seamless single login experience.

13.1 Download IAS Metadata

  1. Open IAS Admin Console → Applications & Resources → Tenant Settings → SAML 2.0 Configuration.
  2. Click Download Metadata File. Save the XML.

13.2 Configure SAML2 in S/4HANA

  1. Run transaction SAML2 in S/4HANA.
  2. Enable SAML 2.0 and create a Local Provider Name.
  3. Trusted Providers tab → Add → Upload Metadata File → upload IAS metadata.
  4. Set NameID format (Login Name recommended — maps to ABAP user ID).
  5. Download S/4HANA SP metadata.

13.3 Register S/4HANA as Application in IAS

  1. IAS Admin Console → Applications → + Add.
  2. Upload S/4HANA SP metadata under Trust → SAML 2.0 Configuration.
  3. Set Subject Name Identifier to match your ABAP user attribute (Login Name).
  4. Save.

Validation: Opening FLP URL redirects to IAS login. After login, FLP opens without ABAP credential prompts. Joule opens without a separate login.

6. Troubleshooting

Symptom

Likely Cause

Fix

Joule icon not visible in FLP

Shell plugin not loaded or role not assigned

Verify role Z_JOULE_S4HANA is assigned. Hard-reload FLP (Ctrl+Shift+R). Check browser console for plugin errors.

'File not found' when Joule loads

Trailing slash in plugin URL

Remove trailing slash: use .../webclient/s4 not .../webclient/s4/.

'Refused to frame/connect' error

Missing Trusted Domain entry

Add the exact blocked origin (shown in browser console) to BTP and IAS Trusted Domains.

Work Zone Channel: Connectivity Error

Real-time callbacks not supported (on-premise)

If status shows 'Updated' with Connectivity Error — content synced. Re-sync manually as needed.

Work Zone 'authorization error' on fetch

Destination URL missing CDM3 path

Ensure destination URL is http://<host>:<port>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3.

Booster fails: 'System already part of formation'

IAS can only be in one Joule formation

Find the existing Joule formation and add your systems to it instead.

SCC mapping 'Not Reachable'

Firewall blocking the port

Ask network team to open the port between SCC and ABAP server. Try internal IP instead of hostname.

Joule opens but prompts for login

IAS SSO not configured for FLP

Complete Step 13 — configure SAML2 trust between S/4HANA and IAS.

Work Zone destination name error

Name has uppercase or underscores

Rename destination to lowercase with no underscores (e.g., jouletc).

Wrong Joule URL after S/4HANA 2025 upgrade

DAS_WEBCLIENT_PLUGIN set to pilot URL

Update /UI2/FLP_SYS_CONF (SM30) with your tenant URL, or remove the parameter.

7. Summary — What We Achieved

  • Joule provisioned on SAP BTP and linked to IAS and Work Zone via a Formation
  • SAP Cloud Connector established as the secure bridge between BTP and S/4HANA
  • Work Zone Channel Manager syncing S/4HANA Fiori catalogs to BTP
  • BTP Destinations correctly routing Work Zone to S/4HANA via Cloud Connector
  • Joule Shell Plugin configured in S/4HANA FLP via a Technical Catalog and PFCG role
  • Joule icon visible and functional in the S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad

entry.title: Handling Large Volumes of Data Between SAP CPI and S/4 HANA
entry.content:
Most SAP CPI integrations work perfectly in development. Then you hit production — and everything changes.

500,000 sales orders. 2 million material records. Suddenly you're seeing timeouts, memory errors, and an S/4 HANA system that slows down for every other user. I've been there. Here's what actually works.

The Real Problem Is End-to-End, Not Just CPI

High-volume data processing touches four layers simultaneously — the ABAP database, the OData gateway, the Cloud Connector tunnel, and the CPI runtime. Most teams focus only on CPI and wonder why tuning iFlow settings doesn't fix anything. The answer is usually one layer below.

Layer 1 — Fix S/4 HANA First

Before writing a single iFlow step, go to your S/4 HANA system and check one thing: are your filter fields indexed?

When CPI filters OData results by LastChangeDateTime, the ABAP layer runs a SQL WHERE clause against the underlying table. Without a database index on that field, the database scans every single row — all 10 million of them — just to return 500 records.

That's the difference between a 0.3-second query and a 15-second one. Across 2,000 pages of data, that's 10 minutes versus 8 hours — while blocking ABAP work processes for every other user on the system.

Check indexes in SE11 → table name → Indexes tab. If your filter field isn't there, raise it with your BASIS team before anything else.

Also verify:

  • OData service is active in SICF and /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE
  • A dedicated technical user exists with the correct S_SERVICE role
  • Test the full OData call with $top=500 in /IWFND/GW_CLIENT before building

Layer 2 — Tune the Cloud Connector

The default connection pool in SAP Cloud Connector is 5 connections. For high-volume integration, that's a bottleneck you can't see — requests queue silently and your iFlow slows without throwing errors.

Increase the pool to 20–50 connections. Enable HTTP Keep-Alive. Enable GZIP compression on the system mapping — this reduces OData payload size by 60–70% and takes load off the tunnel significantly.

Layer 3 — Stop Fighting the OData Adapter

Here's the mistake I see most often: developers manually add $top=500&$skip=0 to the query string, then also enable Process in Pages in the OData adapter. The adapter adds its own pagination on top — values conflict, and you get duplicated pages or an infinite loop on page 1.

The CPI OData Receiver Adapter handles pagination natively. Enable Process in Pages, set your page size (200–500 records), and put only this in your query options:

  • $select — fetch only the fields you need (cuts payload by 60–80%)
  • $filter — for delta loading by date or key field

That's it. No $top. No $skip. Let the adapter do its job.

The Delta Load Pattern — Non-Negotiable at Scale

Never run full loads on recurring integrations with more than 10,000 records. Instead:

  1. Save the run timestamp to a CPI DataStore after each successful execution
  2. On the next run, filter with $filter=LastChangeDateTime gt datetime'<saved timestamp>'
  3. Process only what changed — update the timestamp on success

This turns millions of records into hundreds per run.

The Pattern That Holds at Production Scale

Scheduler

  → Read timestamp from DataStore

  → OData Adapter (Process in Pages ON)

  → Splitter (one record per message)

  → Process + write to target

  → Save new timestamp to DataStore

Simple. Resilient. Scalable.

Here is the skeletal working flow between S/4 OnPrem to CPI to pull data using Get.

  • Timer: OnDeployment
  • Content Modifier4:

Use timestamp variable to fetch data based on specific specific date

property timestamp to be given based on the abap field timestamp like(20000203062111)

 

 

  • Add looping process call
  1. Expression Type : Non XML
  2. Condition Expression: use ${property.Receiver1.OData.hasMoreRecords} is a CPI-internal exchange property that the OData Receiver Adapter sets automatically after each page call.
  3. Max. Numbers of Iterations: 10 (depends on the payload use this effectively, if you expect to receive 1lakh record use 100 to 120)
  4. Action When Max Iterations Reached: Throw Exception

 

 

 

  • add Request/Reply pallet to connect ODATA receiver adapter.
  • use ODATA V2 receiver Adapter

 

 

  1. For my testing using Page Size as 30
  2. Process in pages : checked

In the query there is no need of using $TOP and $SKIP, just make sure these are enabled in ABAP end that’s it,

CPI will take care in calling the looping process call until
Receiver1.OData.hasMoreRecords = ‘false’

 

 

 

 

  • Add ContentModifier2

Every new OData call replaces in.body completely. By the time the loop ends, you only have the last page — all previous pages are lost.

You are accumulating each page response into a property before the next OData call overwrites in.body.

Property name: appendPayload

SourceType: Expression
SourceValue: ${property.appendPayload}${in.body}

 

 

  • Add Content Modifier 3
  1. Under Main integration process add the property appendPayload to the Message body as ${property.appendPayload} to send it to target.
  2. Use all the transformational pallet functions under the Local Integration Process where ODATA is calling to S/4, so that pallet functions process data page wise, at the end you can append payload using the below approach.

 

Lets deploy and monitor

  • CPI Helper

 

  1. CamelLoopIndex : 3

is a built-in CPI property that tells you which iteration the loop is currently on.

  1. For the last looping index got Receiver1.OData.hasMoreRecords = false, next loop will end the process.
  2. SkipRecords_Receiver1.OData: is the property CPI uses internally to track and inject the $skip value into every OData page request — it increments automatically so you never need to calculate it yourself.

 

 

SkipRecords_Receiver1.OData

 

High-volume SAP CPI integration isn't complicated once you understand each layer's role. The teams that struggle are the ones treating it as a black box — tuning CPI while a missing database index silently kills performance.

Know your stack. Test each layer. Let the tools do what they're designed to do.

Have you faced high-volume integration challenges in your SAP landscape? Drop a comment — I'd love to hear your approach.

#SAP #SAPCPI #S4HANA #CloudIntegration #SAPIntegration #OData #CloudConnector #SAPDeveloper #IntegrationArchitecture

entry.title: SAP Developer News, June 11th, 2026
entry.content:
Items

CAP Customer Roundtable 17 June

  • Blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-cloud-application-programming-model-cap-customer-roundtable-june-2026/ba-p/14409654 
  • Teams meeting link https://teams.microsoft.com/meet/326256808750220?p=TOjOLvKwt79TaDUmog%20 
  • Meeting Request Download link https://cap.cloud.sap/resources/events/CAP_Customer_Roundtable_%20June_2026.ics 

Terraform Provider updates 

  • Terraform Provider for SAP BTP https://registry.terraform.io/providers/SAP/btp/latest  
  • Terraform Provider for SAP Cloud Connector https://registry.terraform.io/providers/SAP/scc/latest  

Teaching AI Agents Best Practices: A Skills Workspace for CAP Development 

  • Blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/teaching-ai-agents-best-practices-a-skills-workspace-for-cap-development/ba-p/14414367  

n8n plugin for CAP 

  • Repo on GitHub https://github.com/sleibach/cap-n8n
  • Post on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/posts/soeren-leibach_cap-now-speaks-n8n-annotations-in-workflows-share-7470229600531431425-SUkY

Did You Know? #20 cds up 

  • Capire documentation topic “Deployment” https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/deploy/  

Chapter titles  

00:00 Intro 
00:07 CAP Customer Roundtable 
00:52 Terraform Provider updates 
02:02 Teaching AI Agents Best Practices: A Skills Workspace for CAP Development
02:51 n8n plugin for CAP
04:22 Did You Know? #20 cds up

Transcript

[Intro]
This is the SAP Developer News for June the 11th, 2026.

[Ajay]
Hello, everyone. The second Cloud Application Programming Model Roundtable for 2026 is just around the corner. It is scheduled on Wednesday, June 17, 2026 at 10 a.m. CEST. It is your opportunity to engage directly with the CAP product team and fellow practitioners to discuss recent developments and future directions. The session will cover the latest CAP announcements and updates, product demos and presentations, and an open forum for your topics, questions and discussions. Join us this June to reconnect with CAP community, Exchange Insights, and get ready for reCAP request and meeting request download will be in the description.

[DJ]
Here's some news for all of us platform and infrastructure folks the Terraform provider teams at SAP have been hard at work and have released new versions of the Terraform Providers for SAP BTP and for SAP Cloud Connector. Release 1.23 of the Terraform Provider for SAP BTP brings improvements across multiple resources, and there's also a new help action for easy self-assignment to the subaccount admin role. Released the SAP Cloud Connector contains improvements that include making it easier to work with self-signed certificates and internal certificate authorities, includes guidance on various real-world topics and also has fixes that result in more predictable plans helping prevent configuration drift. Check out the latest releases on the Terraform Provider pages. Links in the description.

[Kevin]
If you're using AI coding agents with SAP CAP, there is a new SAP Community blog post worth reading. Daniel Schlachter introduces a skills workspace approach that helps teach AI agents your team's CAP best practices, conventions, and architectural patterns. Instead of relying on prompts alone, the idea is to create reusable skills that agents can apply consistently across projects. This leads to more predictable production-ready code and makes it easier to scale AI -assisted development within teams. As AI development evolves, success is becoming less about prompting and more about teaching agents how your organization works. Check out the full blog post on SAP Community.

[Daniel]
A new open source CAP plugin was published this week that makes it easy to integrate n8n workflows inside your CAP project. Soren Leibach, a very important SAP BTP architect from the German consulting company Adesso, published into GitHub cap-n8n, wrapping n8n webhook calls for CAP developers. Essentially, you can set up an n8n workflow with webhook triggers, and then from within your CAP application, using annotations, trigger that workflow any time a CAP event occurs, for example, when an entity is saved. You can also programmatically trigger that workflow anywhere in your code inside your CAP application. All you need to provide is a base URL for your n8n system, an API key, and the name of the workflow. There's also test code so that you can set up a local installation of n8n and then try out the CAP plugin. Just a reminder SAP and n8n are in a partnership to embed n8n within the new and improved Joule Studio so you'll be seeing a lot more of n8n in the SAP stack. I'll leave a link to Soren's repo so you can try it out for yourself.

[Nico]
Did you know that you can conveniently build and deploy your CAP applications with a single command? cds up is all you need to build your application locally and deploy to the cloud. And this works for both Cloud Foundry and Kyma or Kubernetes. Let's look at this example. I'm in my CAP project root and I have the respective tooling for my deployment target installed on the machine. That is the Cloud MTA build tool or MBT for short, as well as the Cloud Foundry CLI. If we now execute cds up, we can see that it essentially automates these steps of adding deployment configuration, maybe even multi-tenancy, installing and freezing dependencies, as well as finally building and deploying the application. And remember, you can always get help for any command with the cds CLI by appending --help to the command. Hope this --helps! And bye!

entry.title: Using RFC Function Modules and BAPIs in SAP Build Process Automation
entry.content:
Hi Everyone!

Recently, I was working on a requirement to generate an SD Billing Document in SAP S/4HANA using SAP Build Process Automation.

Due to some internal requirements & technical restrictions, the only option available to me was to use the BAPI_BILLINGDOC_CREATEMULTIPLE BAPI to create the billing document.

To use this BAPI in SAP Build, there are two main configurations required:

1. Map the virtual host to the internal host in SAP Cloud Connector using the RFC protocol, and expose the required BAPI or RFC function module.

2. Create a destination of type RFC in the SAP BTP Cockpit.

Let's first see how Cloud to On-Premises mapping in Cloud Connector looks like:

We also need to enable the required resources, such as the BAPIs and function modules that we want to consume in SAP Build. The RFC_FUNCTION_SEARCH function module enables the search for function modules from SAP Build.



And this is how the destination in BTP looks:

The virtual message server is configured in the Cloud Connector and mapped to the internal SAP S/4HANA host, and logon group information is available in SMLG.



Logon group can be found here :

Now that the Cloud Connector and BTP destination configurations are complete, let’s log in to SAP Build and see how we can create an action using BAPI_BILLINGDOC_CREATEMULTIPLE.

Select RFC's, BAPI's:



All the BAPIs & RFCs from backend will be available to use. We will go ahead and look for BAPI/RFC we'd like to use, and create a new project.

The action is now available in Connectors -> Actions, and can be used in our build projects.



Using this action, I created a custom process that can be triggered to generate a billing document by entering the reference sales document number.



Let's see it in action;
Assume we have below sales order, for which we'd like to generate a billing document.



Let’s now access the custom process we created. I have added the form URL to SAP Build Work Zone for easy access.




Since I added an email notification step to the process, we receive an email once the billing document is generated successfully.



Let's validated the generated billing document number in the system.



That concludes the demo. Thanks for reading!

Stay tuned for more demos on AI agents using Joule Studio, n8n, and automation in SAP Build.

entry.title: Building CPI Explorer – My Journey to Simplify Searching SAP Integration Suite iFlows
entry.content:
Building CPI Explorer – My Journey to Simplify Searching SAP Integration Suite iFlows

Introduction

As an SAP Integration Suite consultant, a large part of my day involves supporting existing integrations, troubleshooting production issues, and understanding how different systems are connected. One challenge kept coming up repeatedly: finding where a particular SFTP directory, HTTP endpoint, ProcessDirect endpoint, or credential was configured across dozens (sometimes hundreds) of Integration Flows.

SAP's Design-Time APIs let you retrieve Integration Packages and Integration Flows, but there's no built-in way to search adapter configurations across an entire tenant. In practice, that meant opening package after package and manually inspecting each iFlow — slow, repetitive, and easy to get wrong in a large landscape.

Instead of continuing to do this by hand, I decided to build a tool to solve it for myself, and hopefully for other developers too.

Where It All Started

I started my career as an ABAP developer, and one feature I relied on constantly was the "Where Used" list. If I needed to know where a table, function module, or field was being used, I could just look it up. It was simple, fast, and always available.

When I moved into PI/PO and later CPI, that convenience disappeared. There's no equivalent way to instantly search where something is used across your integration landscape.

This became a real problem in day-to-day support. An FICO or SD colleague would come to me and say "my file isn't moving, can you check?" Half the time, they weren't even sure whether their interface was flowing through PI/CPI at all — they just knew something was stuck and assumed I'd know where to look. Other times, someone from the infra team would ask "are we using this host anywhere? We need to update it" or "can you find every flow using this username, we're rotating credentials." Each of these meant manually opening iFlow after iFlow, hoping I didn't miss one.

That's where the idea for CPI Explorer actually came from — not as a big architectural vision, but as a way to get back something as basic as the ABAP "Where Used" list, just for CPI.

One real example was during an infrastructure change where we had to update an SFTP host. Before CPI Explorer, the only option was to manually open multiple Integration Flows and inspect every SFTP adapter. With CPI Explorer, I can simply search for the hostname and immediately see every Integration Flow that references it.

The Idea

The question I started with was simple:

 

                   Can I build a "Where Used" list for CPI?

I spent quite some time exploring the SAP Business Accelerator Hub and Design-Time APIs to see whether this functionality already existed. The Design-Time APIs gave me metadata, but not the adapter-level detail I needed. So I went a level deeper: download each Integration Flow artifact, extract the .iflw file, parse the XML, and build a local searchable index from it.

Once that was working, I wrapped it in a Chrome Extension so I could search directly from the browser instead of running scripts every time.

How CPI Explorer Works

At a high level, the tool:

  • Authenticates with SAP Integration Suite using OAuth
  • Retrieves Integration Packages and Integration Flows via the Design-Time APIs
  • Downloads Integration Flow artifacts
  • Extracts and parses the .iflw XML files
  • Builds a searchable metadata index
  • Lets you search adapter properties from the Chrome Extension

It currently supports searching for:

  • SFTP directories
  • Host names
  • HTTP endpoints
  • ProcessDirect endpoints
  • JMS queues
  • Credentials
  • Externalized parameters
  • Other adapter properties

What I Learned

Building this taught me more than I expected about:

  • SAP Integration Suite Design-Time APIs
  • The structure of Integration Flow artifacts
  • Parsing .iflw XML files
  • Chrome Extension development
  • Organizing a Node.js application into reusable modules

The trickiest part was making sure every downloaded artifact mapped back correctly to its source Integration Flow while the index was being built. Getting that mapping right was what made the search results trustworthy across a whole tenant.

Why I'm Sharing This

I built CPI Explorer to solve my own problem, but I believe many SAP Integration Suite developers face the same challenge. That's why I decided to make it open source, so others can use it, contribute ideas, and help improve it over time.

GitHub

The project is available here: https://github.com/lokeshbhukya2019-netizen/cpi-explorer

Feedback, suggestions, and contributions are welcome.

What's Next

A few things I'm planning to add:

  • Search by adapter type
  • Multi-tenant support
  • Export to Excel/CSV
  • Dependency visualization
  • Support for additional adapter types

If you have ideas for other useful features, I'd like to hear them.

Closing Thoughts

CPI Explorer started as an attempt to fix a problem I ran into constantly in day-to-day SAP Integration Suite work. Along the way it turned into a good excuse to learn more about SAP's APIs, artifact structures, and browser extension development.

I hope it saves other SAP Integration Suite developers some time during troubleshooting and impact analysis. If you try it, I'd genuinely like to hear how it goes.

Thank you for taking the time to read about CPI Explorer. If you try it out, I'd love to hear your feedback, suggestions, or ideas for new features. I hope it helps make troubleshooting and impact analysis in SAP Integration Suite a little easier for the community.

Happy integrating!
